jaculatoria.es: Cruz Santa, sÃ© mi luz; no sea el dragÃ³n mi guÃ­a. Ora et Labora.
jaculatoria.en: Holy Cross, be my light; let not the dragon be my guide. Ora et Labora.

prayer.es:
San Benito, padre del monacato occidental, tÃº que encontraste a Dios en el silencio de una cueva en Subiaco y luego enseÃ±aste a otros a encontrarlo en el ritmo diario de la oraciÃ³n y el trabajo â€”

ayÃºdame a descubrir el orden sagrado que puede haber en mi propia vida. No la perfecciÃ³n imposible, sino la fidelidad sencilla: la oraciÃ³n en sus horas, el trabajo con las manos y la mente, el descanso sin culpa, la hospitalidad con el que llega.

TÃº que dijiste Â«Ora et LaboraÂ» â€” Ora y Trabaja â€” recuÃ©rdame que las dos cosas no se oponen: que el trabajo bien hecho es una forma de oraciÃ³n, y que la oraciÃ³n bien vivida da sentido al trabajo.

TÃº que escribiste una Regla de apenas setenta y tres capÃ­tulos y con ella ordenaste la vida de millones durante quince siglos â€” muÃ©strame que la santidad no requiere gestos extraordinarios sino estructura, constancia y amor ordinario.

San Benito de Nursia, Patrono de Europa y Padre de los monjes de Occidente: ruega por nosotros.
AmÃ©n.
prayer.en:
Saint Benedict, father of Western monasticism, you who found God in the silence of a cave in Subiaco and then taught others to find him in the daily rhythm of prayer and work â€”

help me to discover the sacred order that can exist in my own life. Not impossible perfection, but simple fidelity: prayer at its hours, work with hands and mind, rest without guilt, hospitality for the one who arrives.

You who said "Ora et Labora" â€” Pray and Work â€” remind me that the two are not opposed: that work well done is a form of prayer, and that prayer well lived gives meaning to work.

You who wrote a Rule of barely seventy-three chapters and with it ordered the life of millions for fifteen centuries â€” show me that holiness requires not extraordinary gestures but structure, constancy, and ordinary love.

Saint Benedict of Nursia, Patron of Europe and Father of the monks of the West: pray for us.
Amen.

history.es:
Benito naciÃ³ alrededor del aÃ±o 480 en Nursia (hoy Norcia), en la UmbrÃ­a italiana, en el seno de una familia noble. TenÃ­a una hermana gemela, EscolÃ¡stica, que tambiÃ©n serÃ­a santa. Su familia lo enviÃ³ a estudiar a Roma, pero la corrupciÃ³n de la ciudad y la vida disipada de sus compaÃ±eros le causaron tal repulsiÃ³n que, siendo todavÃ­a adolescente, abandonÃ³ los estudios y se retirÃ³ a la soledad.

Se instalÃ³ primero en Enfide (hoy Affile) y luego, guiado por un monje llamado Romano, se fue a vivir como ermitaÃ±o en una cueva inaccesible en las gargantas del rÃ­o Aniene, cerca de Subiaco. AllÃ­ viviÃ³ tres aÃ±os en soledad, oraciÃ³n y penitencia. La fama de su santidad comenzÃ³ a extenderse, y grupos de monjes empezaron a pedirle que los dirigiera. FundÃ³ doce pequeÃ±os monasterios en los alrededores de Subiaco antes de trasladarse, alrededor del aÃ±o 529, a la cima del Monte Cassino, donde fundÃ³ el gran monasterio que lleva su nombre y que serÃ­a el corazÃ³n del monacato occidental.

Fue en Monte Cassino donde escribiÃ³ la Regla de San Benito, un texto de setenta y tres capÃ­tulos breves que describe cÃ³mo debe organizarse la vida de una comunidad monÃ¡stica. La Regla es notable por su equilibrio: distribuye el dÃ­a entre la Liturgia de las Horas (Opus Dei â€” Â«la obra de DiosÂ»), el trabajo manual, la lectio divina y el descanso. Es exigente en lo espiritual, pero tambiÃ©n humana: prescribe que el horario se adapte a los enfermos, los niÃ±os y los mayores; que el abad escuche a los mÃ¡s jÃ³venes antes de tomar decisiones; que se trate a los huÃ©spedes como a Cristo mismo.

Esta Regla sobreviviÃ³ a las invasiones bÃ¡rbaras, a las guerras, a los siglos de oscuridad medieval. Los monjes benedictinos conservaron la cultura clÃ¡sica en sus scriptoria, cultivaron la tierra, establecieron escuelas, fundaron hospitales y mantuvieron la fe en zonas que de otro modo habrÃ­an quedado sin presencia cristiana. Se calcula que de la Regla de San Benito y de sus monasterios surgieron mÃ¡s de cincuenta mil fundaciones a lo largo de los siglos.

Benito muriÃ³ en Monte Cassino alrededor del aÃ±o 547. SegÃºn el relato de San Gregorio Magno â€” nuestra principal fuente biogrÃ¡fica â€” muriÃ³ de pie, sostenido por sus monjes, despuÃ©s de recibir el ViÃ¡tico, con los ojos levantados al cielo y los brazos extendidos en oraciÃ³n.

El papa Pablo VI lo proclamÃ³ Patrono de Europa en 1964, reconociendo que la civilizaciÃ³n europea, tal como la conocemos, fue en gran parte configurada por los monasterios benedictinos. Su sÃ­mbolo mÃ¡s conocido es la Medalla de San Benito, con la inscripciÃ³n CRUX SANCTI PATRIS BENEDICTI (Â«Cruz del Santo Padre BenitoÂ»), usada por millones de fieles como protecciÃ³n contra el mal. Sus otros atributos son el cuervo (que, segÃºn la leyenda, transportÃ³ lejos un pan envenenado que un presbÃ­tero celoso habÃ­a enviado a Benito para matarlo) y la copa rota o el cÃ¡liz con una serpiente (que alude al mismo intento de envenenamiento: el vaso se rompiÃ³ milagrosamente cuando Benito hizo la seÃ±al de la cruz sobre Ã©l).
history.en:
Benedict was born around 480 in Nursia (today Norcia), in Umbria, Italy, into a noble family. He had a twin sister, Scholastica, who would also become a saint. His family sent him to study in Rome, but the corruption of the city and the dissolute life of his companions caused him such revulsion that, still an adolescent, he abandoned his studies and withdrew into solitude.

He settled first in Enfide (today Affile), and then, guided by a monk named Romanus, went to live as a hermit in an inaccessible cave in the gorges of the Aniene River near Subiaco. There he lived three years in solitude, prayer, and penance. The fame of his holiness began to spread, and groups of monks started asking him to lead them. He founded twelve small monasteries in the area around Subiaco before moving, around 529, to the summit of Monte Cassino, where he founded the great monastery that bears his name and would become the heart of Western monasticism.

It was at Monte Cassino that he wrote the Rule of Saint Benedict, a text of seventy-three brief chapters describing how the life of a monastic community should be organized. The Rule is notable for its balance: it distributes the day between the Liturgy of the Hours (Opus Dei â€” "the work of God"), manual labor, lectio divina, and rest. It is demanding spiritually, but also human: it prescribes that the schedule be adapted for the sick, children, and the elderly; that the abbot listen to the youngest before making decisions; that guests be treated as Christ himself.

This Rule survived the barbarian invasions, the wars, the centuries of medieval darkness. Benedictine monks preserved classical culture in their scriptoria, cultivated the land, established schools, founded hospitals, and maintained the faith in areas that would otherwise have been left without Christian presence. It is estimated that from the Rule of Saint Benedict and his monasteries more than fifty thousand foundations emerged over the centuries.

Benedict died at Monte Cassino around 547. According to the account of Saint Gregory the Great â€” our primary biographical source â€” he died standing upright, supported by his monks, after receiving Viaticum, with his eyes raised to heaven and his arms extended in prayer.

Pope Paul VI proclaimed him Patron of Europe in 1964, recognizing that European civilization, as we know it, was largely shaped by Benedictine monasteries. His best-known symbol is the Medal of Saint Benedict, with the inscription CRUX SANCTI PATRIS BENEDICTI ("Cross of the Holy Father Benedict"), used by millions of the faithful as protection against evil. His other attributes are the raven (which, according to legend, carried away a poisoned loaf that a jealous priest had sent to Benedict to kill him) and the broken cup or chalice with a serpent (alluding to the same poisoning attempt: the vessel shattered miraculously when Benedict made the sign of the cross over it).

novena_prayers.es:
Esta novena honra a San Benito como padre del monacato, maestro del equilibrio espiritual y Patrono de Europa. Es especialmente adecuada para quienes buscan orden en su vida de fe, quienes atraviesan momentos de tentaciÃ³n o peligro espiritual, y quienes desean integrar oraciÃ³n y trabajo cotidiano.

Cada dÃ­a:
1. Reza 1 Padre Nuestro, 1 Ave MarÃ­a y 1 Gloria.
2. Lee la meditaciÃ³n del dÃ­a.
3. Concluye con: Â«San Benito de Nursia, Patrono de Europa y padre de los monjes: ruega por nosotros y por todos los que buscan a Dios en el silencio.Â»
novena_prayers.en:
This novena honors Saint Benedict as the father of monasticism, master of spiritual balance, and Patron of Europe. It is especially suitable for those seeking order in their life of faith, those going through moments of temptation or spiritual danger, and those wishing to integrate prayer and daily work.

Each day:
1. Pray 1 Our Father, 1 Hail Mary, and 1 Glory Be.
2. Read the day's meditation.
3. Close with: "Saint Benedict of Nursia, Patron of Europe and father of the monks: pray for us and for all who seek God in silence."

novena.es:
DÃ­a 1 â€” Ora et Labora

San Benito, tu lema â€” Ora et Labora, Ora y Trabaja â€” organizÃ³ la vida de millones. No decÃ­as que la oraciÃ³n era para los contemplativos y el trabajo para los activos: decÃ­as que ambas cosas pertenecen a toda vida humana, que se alternan y se complementan.

AyÃºdame a ver mi trabajo como oraciÃ³n y mi oraciÃ³n como el fundamento que da sentido al trabajo. Que ninguno de los dos quede separado del otro en mi vida.

San Benito, ruega por nosotros.
Reza: 1 Padre Nuestro, 1 Ave MarÃ­a, 1 Gloria.
novena.en:
Day 1 â€” Ora et Labora

Saint Benedict, your motto â€” Ora et Labora, Pray and Work â€” organized the lives of millions. You did not say that prayer was for contemplatives and work for activists: you said that both things belong to every human life, that they alternate and complement each other.

Help me to see my work as prayer and my prayer as the foundation that gives meaning to work. May neither remain separated from the other in my life.

Saint Benedict, pray for us.
Pray: 1 Our Father, 1 Hail Mary, 1 Glory Be.

novena.es:
DÃ­a 2 â€” El Silencio de Subiaco

San Benito, antes de fundar monasterios pasaste tres aÃ±os en una cueva en Subiaco. Solo, en silencio, sin audiencia ni reconocimiento. Era el tiempo de conocerte a ti mismo y conocer a Dios sin intermediarios.

En un mundo que premia la visibilidad y castiga el retiro, ayÃºdame a no tener miedo al silencio. A reservar cada dÃ­a, aunque sea un breve momento, para escuchar lo que solo se escucha en el silencio.

San Benito, ruega por nosotros.
Reza: 1 Padre Nuestro, 1 Ave MarÃ­a, 1 Gloria.
novena.en:
Day 2 â€” The Silence of Subiaco

Saint Benedict, before founding monasteries you spent three years in a cave in Subiaco. Alone, in silence, without audience or recognition. It was the time to know yourself and know God without intermediaries.

In a world that rewards visibility and punishes withdrawal, help me not to fear silence. To reserve each day, even if just a brief moment, to listen to what can only be heard in silence.

Saint Benedict, pray for us.
Pray: 1 Our Father, 1 Hail Mary, 1 Glory Be.

novena.es:
DÃ­a 3 â€” La Regla: Estructura como Libertad

San Benito, tu Regla no es una lista de prohibiciones sino un horario de libertad. Distribuir el dÃ­a entre oraciÃ³n, trabajo y descanso es una forma de proteger cada uno de los tres de los excesos de los otros. Una vida sin estructura tiende a consumirse en lo urgente y olvidar lo importante.

AyÃºdame a construir un orden en mi vida que no sea rigidez sino andamiaje: algo que me sostenga sin aplastarme, que me libere de tener que improvisar cada maÃ±ana desde cero.

San Benito, ruega por nosotros.
Reza: 1 Padre Nuestro, 1 Ave MarÃ­a, 1 Gloria.
novena.en:
Day 3 â€” The Rule: Structure as Freedom

Saint Benedict, your Rule is not a list of prohibitions but a schedule of freedom. Distributing the day between prayer, work, and rest is a way of protecting each of the three from the excesses of the others. A life without structure tends to be consumed by the urgent and forget the important.

Help me to build an order in my life that is not rigidity but scaffolding: something that supports me without crushing me, that frees me from having to improvise each morning from scratch.

Saint Benedict, pray for us.
Pray: 1 Our Father, 1 Hail Mary, 1 Glory Be.

novena.es:
DÃ­a 4 â€” El HuÃ©sped como Cristo

San Benito, en tu Regla escribiste: Â«Se recibirÃ¡ a todos los huÃ©spedes como a CristoÂ». No a los huÃ©spedes merecedores, no a los agradables ni a los que comparten nuestra fe: a todos. Porque en el otro, incluso en el extraÃ±o, se esconde el rostro de Cristo.

AyÃºdame a ver asÃ­ al que llega a mi vida inesperadamente. A no filtrar con demasiada rapidez quiÃ©n merece mi atenciÃ³n. Que la hospitalidad no sea solo virtud social sino forma de fe.

San Benito, ruega por nosotros.
Reza: 1 Padre Nuestro, 1 Ave MarÃ­a, 1 Gloria.
novena.en:
Day 4 â€” The Guest as Christ

Saint Benedict, in your Rule you wrote: "All guests who arrive shall be received as Christ." Not deserving guests, not pleasant ones or those who share our faith: all. Because in the other, even in the stranger, the face of Christ lies hidden.

Help me to see in this way the one who arrives unexpectedly in my life. Not to filter too quickly who deserves my attention. May hospitality be not only social virtue but a form of faith.

Saint Benedict, pray for us.
Pray: 1 Our Father, 1 Hail Mary, 1 Glory Be.

novena.es:
DÃ­a 5 â€” El Abad Escucha al Joven

San Benito, prescribiste que el abad convoque a toda la comunidad antes de tomar decisiones importantes, Â«porque a veces el SeÃ±or revela al mÃ¡s joven lo que es mejorÂ». Era una humildad institucional: reconocer que la sabidurÃ­a no es monopolio de la edad ni del cargo.

AyÃºdame a esa misma apertura: a escuchar antes de decidir, a no descalificar una voz por ser joven o desconocida, a reconocer que Dios puede hablar por cualquier canal.

San Benito, ruega por nosotros.
Reza: 1 Padre Nuestro, 1 Ave MarÃ­a, 1 Gloria.
novena.en:
Day 5 â€” The Abbot Listens to the Young

Saint Benedict, you prescribed that the abbot call together the whole community before making important decisions, "because sometimes the Lord reveals to the youngest what is better." It was an institutional humility: recognizing that wisdom is not the monopoly of age or office.

Help me to that same openness: to listen before deciding, not to disqualify a voice for being young or unknown, to recognize that God can speak through any channel.

Saint Benedict, pray for us.
Pray: 1 Our Father, 1 Hail Mary, 1 Glory Be.

novena.es:
DÃ­a 6 â€” La TentaciÃ³n y la Cueva

San Benito, la leyenda cuenta que en tu cueva de Subiaco enfrentaste tentaciones de lujuria tan violentas que te lanzaste a las zarzas para calmarlas con el dolor fÃ­sico. No te da vergÃ¼enza que lo sepamos: tu vida entera es prueba de que la santidad no es ausencia de tentaciÃ³n sino victoria sobre ella.

AyÃºdame en mis propias tentaciones a no rendirme sin pelear. A saber que el hecho de ser tentado no me hace menos hijo de Dios. Y que la victoria no tiene que ser definitiva para cada batalla sino que basta seguir combatiendo.

San Benito, ruega por nosotros.
Reza: 1 Padre Nuestro, 1 Ave MarÃ­a, 1 Gloria.
novena.en:
Day 6 â€” Temptation and the Cave

Saint Benedict, legend tells that in your cave at Subiaco you faced temptations of lust so violent that you threw yourself into thornbushes to calm them with physical pain. You are not ashamed that we know this: your entire life is proof that holiness is not the absence of temptation but victory over it.

Help me in my own temptations not to surrender without fighting. To know that being tempted does not make me less a child of God. And that victory does not have to be definitive for each battle â€” it is enough to keep fighting.

Saint Benedict, pray for us.
Pray: 1 Our Father, 1 Hail Mary, 1 Glory Be.

novena.es:
DÃ­a 7 â€” La Copa Rota

San Benito, cuando un presbÃ­tero celoso te enviÃ³ un vino envenenado, levantaste tu mano para bendecirlo y la copa se rompiÃ³ sola. La leyenda dice que el veneno huye ante la seÃ±al de la cruz.

Medalla de San Benito, que llevan millones: CRUX SANCTI PATRIS BENEDICTI. Que la seÃ±al de la cruz no sea para mÃ­ un gesto automÃ¡tico sino un acto de fe: que creo que el SeÃ±or de la vida tiene mÃ¡s poder que cualquier mal que se me acerque. Que no tengo que enfrentarlo solo.

San Benito, ruega por nosotros.
Reza: 1 Padre Nuestro, 1 Ave MarÃ­a, 1 Gloria.
novena.en:
Day 7 â€” The Broken Cup

Saint Benedict, when a jealous priest sent you poisoned wine, you raised your hand to bless it and the cup broke on its own. Legend says that poison flees before the sign of the cross.

Medal of Saint Benedict, worn by millions: CRUX SANCTI PATRIS BENEDICTI. May the sign of the cross not be for me an automatic gesture but an act of faith: that I believe the Lord of life has more power than any evil that approaches me. That I do not have to face it alone.

Saint Benedict, pray for us.
Pray: 1 Our Father, 1 Hail Mary, 1 Glory Be.

novena.es:
DÃ­a 8 â€” Europa y sus RaÃ­ces

San Benito, el papa Pablo VI te proclamÃ³ Patrono de Europa en 1964. No era nostalgia sino una afirmaciÃ³n: la civilizaciÃ³n europea, sus hospitales, sus universidades, su arte, su preservaciÃ³n del saber antiguo â€” todo eso pasÃ³ en gran parte por los monasterios benedictinos.

Intercede por Europa hoy: por una civilizaciÃ³n que ha olvidado a medias sus raÃ­ces pero que las lleva todavÃ­a en los huesos. Que no haga de la memoria una trampa ni del olvido una liberaciÃ³n, sino que encuentre en sus raÃ­ces la fuerza para afrontar lo nuevo.

San Benito, ruega por nosotros.
Reza: 1 Padre Nuestro, 1 Ave MarÃ­a, 1 Gloria.
novena.en:
Day 8 â€” Europe and Its Roots

Saint Benedict, Pope Paul VI proclaimed you Patron of Europe in 1964. It was not nostalgia but an affirmation: European civilization, its hospitals, its universities, its art, its preservation of ancient knowledge â€” all of that passed in large part through Benedictine monasteries.

Intercede for Europe today: for a civilization that has half-forgotten its roots but still carries them in its bones. May it not make of memory a trap or of forgetting a liberation, but find in its roots the strength to face the new.

Saint Benedict, pray for us.
Pray: 1 Our Father, 1 Hail Mary, 1 Glory Be.

novena.es:
DÃ­a 9 â€” La Muerte de Pie

San Benito, moriste de pie, sostenido por tus monjes, con los ojos al cielo y los brazos extendidos en oraciÃ³n. Igual que habÃ­as vivido: en actitud de oraciÃ³n, de entrega, de apertura.

Intercede por mÃ­ para que aprenda a vivir asÃ­: no solo a morir bien, sino a vivir con esa misma postura interior â€” de pie, con los ojos en alto, con los brazos abiertos. Que mi vida entera sea, en ese sentido, una preparaciÃ³n para la muerte que serÃ¡ tambiÃ©n una oraciÃ³n final.

San Benito de Nursia, padre de los monjes y Patrono de Europa: ruega por nosotros.

Reza: 1 Padre Nuestro, 1 Ave MarÃ­a, 1 Gloria.
novena.en:
Day 9 â€” The Death Standing Upright

Saint Benedict, you died standing upright, supported by your monks, with your eyes toward heaven and your arms extended in prayer. Just as you had lived: in an attitude of prayer, of surrender, of openness.

Intercede for me that I may learn to live thus: not only to die well, but to live with that same inner posture â€” standing, with eyes raised, with arms open. May my entire life be, in that sense, a preparation for a death that will also be a final prayer.

Saint Benedict of Nursia, father of the monks and Patron of Europe: pray for us.

Pray: 1 Our Father, 1 Hail Mary, 1 Glory Be.

miracles.es:
1. EL CÃLIZ QUE SE ROMPIÃ“ ANTE EL VENENO
San Gregorio Magno, que escribiÃ³ la vida de Benito unos cincuenta aÃ±os despuÃ©s de su muerte con base en testimonios directos de sus discÃ­pulos, relata que un sacerdote de la regiÃ³n, Florencio, celoso de la fama del santo, le enviÃ³ un cÃ¡liz con vino envenenado como regalo. Benito, antes de beber, hizo la seÃ±al de la cruz sobre el vaso, que se rompiÃ³ instantÃ¡neamente como si hubiera recibido una pedrada. Benito entendiÃ³ el mensaje, hizo oraciÃ³n por Florencio y se retirÃ³ del lugar para evitar que el odio del sacerdote daÃ±ara a los monjes. La misma noche, Florencio muriÃ³ aplastado por el derrumbe del balcÃ³n desde el que contemplaba la partida de Benito. El relato es la fuente de la iconografÃ­a del cÃ¡liz con la serpiente que acompaÃ±a siempre las representaciones de Benito y de la Medalla de San Benito.

2. LAS RESURRECCIONES NARRADAS POR GREGORIO
La Vita de Gregorio Magno (Libro II de sus DiÃ¡logos) narra al menos dos resurrecciones atribuidas a Benito en vida. La primera: un joven monje muerto en un derrumbe fue llevado a la celda del santo, quien orÃ³ sobre el cuerpo y el joven resucitÃ³. La segunda: el hijo de un campesino fue traÃ­do muerto ante Benito mientras este trabajaba en el campo; el santo se arrojÃ³ sobre el cuerpo, orÃ³, y el niÃ±o volviÃ³ a la vida ante los ojos de su padre. Gregorio, que era un historiador riguroso para su Ã©poca, sitÃºa estos hechos en el contexto de un discernimiento sobre las fuentes: sus informantes fueron discÃ­pulos directos de Benito, incluido el abad Hortensius y el diÃ¡cono Valentinianus.

3. LA FUENTE DE AGUA EN MONTE CASSINO
Al establecerse en Monte Cassino, los monjes sufrÃ­an por la falta de agua: el monasterio estaba en lo alto de la montaÃ±a y acarrear agua desde el valle era un trabajo agotador. Tres monjes subieron al santo una peticiÃ³n: necesitaban agua. Benito pasÃ³ la noche en oraciÃ³n en el lugar indicado, clavÃ³ tres piedras en el suelo y regresÃ³. A la maÃ±ana siguiente, en ese mismo punto brotÃ³ un manantial que â€” segÃºn los benedictinos de Monte Cassino â€” no ha dejado de fluir desde entonces. Este manantial es aÃºn hoy mostrado a los peregrinos en los terrenos del monasterio, reconstruido tras los bombardeos de 1944.
miracles.en:
1. THE CHALICE THAT BROKE BEFORE THE POISON
Saint Gregory the Great, who wrote the life of Benedict about fifty years after his death based on direct testimonies from his disciples, recounts that a priest from the region, Florentius, jealous of the saint's fame, sent him a chalice with poisoned wine as a gift. Benedict, before drinking, made the sign of the cross over the cup, which broke instantly as if struck by a stone. Benedict understood the message, prayed for Florentius, and withdrew from the place to prevent the priest's hatred from harming the monks. That same night, Florentius died crushed by the collapse of the balcony from which he was watching Benedict's departure. The account is the source of the iconography of the chalice with the serpent that always accompanies representations of Benedict and of the Medal of Saint Benedict.

2. THE RESURRECTIONS NARRATED BY GREGORY
Gregory the Great's Vita (Book II of his Dialogues) narrates at least two resurrections attributed to Benedict during his lifetime. The first: a young monk who died in a collapse was brought to the saint's cell; he prayed over the body and the young man was resurrected. The second: the son of a peasant was brought dead before Benedict while he was working in the field; the saint threw himself on the body, prayed, and the child came back to life before the eyes of his father. Gregory, who was a rigorous historian for his time, places these events in the context of a discernment about sources: his informants were direct disciples of Benedict, including Abbot Hortensius and the deacon Valentinianus.

3. THE SPRING OF WATER AT MONTE CASSINO
Upon settling at Monte Cassino, the monks suffered from a lack of water: the monastery was on top of the mountain and carrying water from the valley was exhausting work. Three monks brought the saint a petition: they needed water. Benedict spent the night in prayer at the indicated spot, drove three stones into the ground, and returned. The following morning, at that very point a spring burst forth which â€” according to the Benedictines of Monte Cassino â€” has not stopped flowing since. This spring is still shown today to pilgrims on the grounds of the monastery, rebuilt after the 1944 bombardments.
